segments.inferno-5-g12: "Sempre" sets the habitual, continual nature of the scene. "Dinanzi a lui" means "before him," referring to Minos, and "ne" is partitive, "of them," with "molte" as the feminine plural subject: literally, "many of them stand."
segments.inferno-5-g13: "Vanno" is present tense and continues the description of repeated action. "A vicenda" is an idiom meaning "in turn," while "ciascuna" refers back to the many souls, singularized as "each one" going "al giudizio," "to the judgment."
segments.inferno-5-g14: The coordinated present-tense verbs "dicono e odono" mean that the souls confess or speak and then hear Minos's sentence. "Son" is the poetic contracted form of "sono," and "giù volte" means "turned downward" or "sent down," with "volte" functioning as a past participial idea from "volgere," though the listed lemma gives only noun senses.
segments.inferno-5-g15: "O tu" is a direct apostrophe, "O you," introducing Minos's address to Dante. "Che vieni" is a relative clause, "who come," and "al doloroso ospizio" means "to the sorrowful lodging," a grim, ironic name for the place of damnation.
segments.inferno-5-g16: "Disse Minòs a me" uses normal narrative preterite: "Minos said to me." "Quando mi vide" is a temporal clause, "when he saw me," with "mi" as the direct object of "vide."
segments.inferno-5-g17: "Lasciando" is a gerund, explaining what Minos was doing as he spoke: he was leaving or pausing his usual action. "L’atto di cotanto offizio" means "the act of so great an office," with "cotanto" emphasizing the solemn magnitude of his judicial duty.
segments.inferno-5-g18: "Guarda" is an imperative meaning "take heed" or "beware," not simply "look." "Com’ entri" is elided for "come entri," "how you enter"; "entri" is the verb "you enter," despite the supplied lemma. "Di cui tu ti fide" means "in whom you trust," with "di" governed by the reflexive verb "fidarsi/fidere" and "fide" an archaic form for "you trust."
segments.inferno-5-g19: "Non t’inganni" is a negative jussive/subjunctive construction: literally, "may it not deceive you," or naturally, "do not let it deceive you." The subject follows the verb: "l’ampiezza de l’intrare," "the breadth/spaciousness of the entrance"; "de l’" is the older separated form of modern "dell’."
segments.inferno-5-g20: This segment contains only punctuation and carries no independent grammatical content or lexical sense.
segments.inferno-5-g21: “E” links this speech to the preceding action. “’l” is the contracted form of “il,” and “duca mio” means “my guide”; “a lui” marks the addressee, with the verb of saying left implicit before the quoted words.
segments.inferno-5-g22: “Perché” introduces a direct question meaning “why.” “Pur” here has the force of “still” or “continually,” and “gride” is an archaic/poetic second-person singular form of “gridi,” “you cry out.”
segments.inferno-5-g23: “Non impedir” is a negative command addressed to Minos; “impedir” is the apocopated infinitive used imperatively, equivalent to “do not impede.” “Lo suo fatale andare” uses the archaic article-plus-possessive construction, literally “the his fated going,” with “andare” as a noun meaning his journey or passage.
segments.inferno-5-g24: “Vuolsi” is “vuol + si,” a passive/impersonal form meaning “it is willed”; “così” tells what is willed, “so/thus.” “Colà dove” means “there where,” referring reverently to the place of divine authority, and “si puote ciò che si vuole” is impersonal: “one can do what one wills,” or “what is willed can be done.”
segments.inferno-5-g25: “E” coordinates this final command with the previous prohibition. “Più non dimandare” has the negative adverbial phrase “no longer/no more,” and “dimandare” is an archaic spelling of “domandare,” “to ask.”
segments.inferno-5-g26: “Or” means “now,” marking a new stage in Dante’s perception. “Incomincian” is the shortened third-person plural present “incominciano,” with “le dolenti note” as its subject; “a farmisi sentire” depends on “incomincian” and means “to make themselves heard by me,” with “mi” as the experiencer and “si” reflexive/passive.
segments.inferno-5-g27: The second “or” again means “now,” and “son venuto” is the present perfect with “essere”: “I have come/arrived.” “Là dove” means “there where”; “molto pianto” is the singular subject, “much weeping,” and “mi percuote” says that this weeping strikes or assails Dante.
segments.inferno-5-g1: “Così” links the new action to what has just been narrated: “thus/so.” “Discesi” is first-person singular passato remoto of “discendere,” and “del ... nel” means motion from the first circle down into the second; “primaio” is an archaic/poetic form for “first,” and “secondo” is substantivized, meaning “the second circle.”
segments.inferno-5-g2: “Che” is a relative pronoun referring to “il secondo” circle. The clause has poetic word order: “men loco cinghia” means “girds/encloses less space,” with “loco” as object and “cinghia” an archaic form of “cinge”; “e tanto più dolor” adds the contrasting increase in pain.
segments.inferno-5-g3: Here “che” introduces a result clause after “tanto più dolor”: “so much more pain that...” “Punge” is present indicative and personifies the pain as something that pricks or stings, while “a guaio” gives the result or direction of that sting: toward a cry of woe.
segments.inferno-5-g4: “Stavvi” is “sta + vi,” literally “there stands,” with the locative pronoun attached after the verb in an archaic style. “Minòs” is the subject, and “orribilmente” modifies his manner or appearance as terrifying.
segments.inferno-5-g5: The conjunction “e” coordinates this action with “Stavvi.” “Ringhia” is present indicative, giving a vivid, ongoing description of Minos as he growls or snarls.
segments.inferno-5-g6: “Essamina” is an archaic spelling of “esamina,” present indicative: Minos “examines” or scrutinizes. “Ne l’intrata” corresponds to modern “nell’entrata,” meaning at or in the entrance, where the arriving souls’ “colpe” are assessed.
segments.inferno-5-g7: “Giudica e manda” are coordinated present indicatives: Minos judges the soul and sends it onward. “Secondo ch’avvinghia” means “according as he coils/wraps,” with “ch’” elided before a vowel and referring to the way he winds his tail to indicate the sentence.
segments.inferno-5-g8: “Dico che” introduces Dante’s explanatory statement. The temporal clause begins with “quando”; “l’anima mal nata” means the damned or ill-born soul, and “li vien dinanzi” uses an archaic form where “li” functions as “to him/before him,” not as the modern article.
segments.inferno-5-g9: “Tutta” is adverbial in force here: the soul confesses wholly or completely. “Si confessa” is reflexive/middle in form, meaning that the soul makes a full confession of itself and its sins.
segments.inferno-5-g10: “Quel conoscitor de le peccata” means “that knower of sins,” with “de le” as the older separated form of modern “delle.” “Vede qual loco d’inferno è da essa” has the sense of discerning which place in Hell is assigned to or fitting for the soul; “da essa” here means “for it,” not simply “from it.”
segments.inferno-5-g11: “Cignesi” is an archaic form of “si cinge,” with enclitic “-si”: “he girds/wraps himself.” “Tante volte quantunque gradi” means “as many times as the degrees/levels,” and “vuol che ... sia messa” uses “volere” plus “che” and a subjunctive passive, “sia messa,” meaning “he wants it to be put/sent down.”
segments.inferno-5-g28: “Io venni” is first-person singular passato remoto, the narrative past: “I came.” “In loco” means “into/to a place,” with archaic or poetic “loco” for modern “luogo”; “d’ogne luce muto” places the adjective “muto” after its complement, literally “mute of every light,” meaning deprived of all light.
segments.inferno-5-g29: “Che” is a relative pronoun referring back to “loco”: the place “which roars.” “Mugghia” is present indicative, and “come fa mar” means “as the sea does,” with “fa” standing in for the repeated verb “roars”; “mar” is the poetic shortened form of “mare.”
segments.inferno-5-g30: Here “se” means “if/when,” introducing the condition under which the sea roars like that. “È combattuto” is a passive construction with “essere” plus past participle, “is assailed/beaten,” and “da contrari venti” gives the agent: “by opposing winds.”
segments.inferno-5-g31: This noun phrase is the subject of the sentence. “Infernal” is a truncated poetic form of “infernale,” agreeing with feminine singular “bufera.”
segments.inferno-5-g32: “Che” introduces a relative clause describing “la bufera.” “Mai non resta” uses medieval Italian negative concord: literally “ever not stops,” but meaning “never stops.”
segments.inferno-5-g33: “Mena” is present indicative from “menare,” here meaning “drives” or “carries along,” though the supplied lemma points to a different word. “Li spirti” is an archaic form for “gli spiriti,” and “con la sua rapina” expresses the whirlwind’s violent, snatching force.
segments.inferno-5-g34: “Voltando e percotendo” are gerunds coordinated by “e,” explaining how the whirlwind torments the souls: by turning and striking them. “Li” here is not the article but an object pronoun, “them,” and “molesta” is a present-tense verb, “it troubles/torments.”
segments.inferno-5-g35: “Quando” has a general repeated sense, “whenever.” “Giungon” is the apocopated poetic form of “giungono,” third-person plural present; “davanti a la ruina” means “before the ruin/ruined fall,” with “a la” not yet contracted to modern “alla.”
segments.inferno-5-g36: “Quivi” is an archaic adverb meaning “there.” The verb is omitted by ellipsis: the phrase means “there [are] the shrieks,” beginning a list of sounds heard at that point.
segments.inferno-5-g37: This continues the verbless list begun with “quivi le strida.” “Il compianto” is singular and collective, meaning “the lamentation” or “the mourning.”
segments.inferno-5-g38: This is the final noun in the same elliptical list: “the wailing/lament.” The repeated article “il” gives each sound its own weight in the catalogue.
segments.inferno-5-g39: “Bestemmian” is the apocopated poetic form of “bestemmiano,” third-person plural present: “they blaspheme/curse.” “La virtù divina” means “the divine power,” with “divina” agreeing in gender and number with “virtù.”
segments.inferno-5-g87: This is a narrative tag introducing direct speech: “and he to me,” with the verb of saying understood, a common poetic ellipsis. “Elli” is an archaic form of “egli,” meaning “he,” referring to Virgil.
segments.inferno-5-g88: “Vedrai” is future tense, second-person singular: “you will see.” The clause “quando saranno più presso a noi” means “when they are nearer to us”; “saranno” is future of “essere,” and “più presso” forms the comparative “nearer/closer.”
segments.inferno-5-g89: The subject pronoun “tu” is explicit for emphasis: “you, then, entreat them.” “Li” and “i” are object pronouns meaning “them,” while “per quello amor che i mena” means “by/in the name of that love which leads them,” with “che” as a relative pronoun referring to “amor.”
segments.inferno-5-g90: “Ei” is an archaic plural pronoun here, meaning “they.” “Verranno” is the future tense of “venire,” completing Virgil’s assurance: if Dante calls them rightly, they will come.
segments.inferno-5-g91: “Sì tosto come” is an idiom meaning “as soon as.” “Il vento a noi li piega” has poetic word order: literally, “the wind turns them to us,” where “li” is the direct object pronoun referring to the two souls.
segments.inferno-5-g92: “Mossi” is first-person singular passato remoto from “muovere”: literally “I moved the voice,” an idiom meaning “I raised my voice” or “I began to speak.” “La voce” is the object of the verb.
segments.inferno-5-g93: This is a vocative address: “O anime affannate,” directly calling to the souls. “Affannate” is a feminine plural past participle used adjectivally, agreeing with “anime” and meaning distressed, weary, or tormented.
segments.inferno-5-g94: “Venite” is a second-person plural imperative: “come.” The infinitive “parlar” depends on the motion verb, giving purpose: “come to speak with us” or “come speak with us.”
segments.inferno-5-g95: “S’altri” contracts “se altri,” meaning “if another/someone else.” “Nol” contracts “non lo,” meaning “does not it,” and “niega” here has the force of “forbids”: “if another does not forbid it.”
segments.inferno-5-g96: This segment contains only punctuation, marking the end of the preceding utterance or sentence; there are no words to parse.
segments.inferno-5-g59: This target contains only the punctuation mark ending the preceding speech unit; there are no words or grammatical constructions to analyze here.
segments.inferno-5-g60: “La prima” is a substantivized feminine adjective, “the first woman,” referring to the first soul in the group being identified. “Color” is an apocopated form of “coloro,” “those,” and “di cui novelle tu vuo’ saper” means “about whom you wish to know news/accounts”; “vuo’” is the contracted poetic form of “vuoi,” governing the infinitive “saper.”
segments.inferno-5-g61: “Mi” is dative, “to me,” and “disse” is passato remoto, the narrative past: “said.” “Quelli” means “that one,” referring to the guide, and “allotta” is an archaic form meaning “then” or “at that time.”
segments.inferno-5-g62: “Fu” is passato remoto of “essere,” introducing the predicate noun “imperadrice.” “Di molte favelle” literally means “of many languages/tongues,” a poetic way of saying that she ruled over many peoples or nations.
segments.inferno-5-g63: “A vizio di lussuria” depends on “rotta”: literally “broken to the vice of lust,” meaning figuratively “given over/addicted to lust.” “Fu ... rotta” uses “fu” with a past participle/adjective, and “sì” is the intensifier “so,” preparing the following result clause introduced by “che.”
segments.inferno-5-g64: Here “che” is consecutive after “sì”: “so ... that.” “Libito fé licito” has the compressed word order “she made desire lawful,” with “fé” as the shortened poetic form of “fece”; “in sua legge” means within her own law or legislation.
segments.inferno-5-g65: “Per tòrre” is a purpose phrase: “in order to remove/take away.” “In che era condotta” is a relative construction meaning “into which she had been brought,” with “era condotta” functioning as a passive form; the antecedent is “biasmo,” the blame or disgrace she wished to erase.
segments.inferno-5-g76: “Vedi” is an imperative addressed to Dante: here it means “behold” or “look at,” continuing the guide’s catalogue of famous lovers. “Parìs” is Paris of Troy, the direct object of the verb.
segments.inferno-5-g77: “Tristano” is another direct object of the understood imperative “Vedi.” The name is set off before the quotation closes, completing the list with Tristan, the legendary lover.
segments.inferno-5-g78: “E” links this clause to the preceding list; “più di mille ombre” means “more than a thousand shades,” with “di” used after a comparative. “Mostrommi” and “nominommi” are poetic enclitic forms: “mostrò mi” and “nominò mi,” meaning “he showed me” and “he named to me”; “a dito” means by pointing with a finger.
segments.inferno-5-g79: “Ch’” is a relative pronoun referring back to the “ombre,” “whom/which.” “Dipartille” is “dipartì le,” with the object pronoun attached to the verb: literally “separated them,” here meaning that love cut them off from earthly life.
segments.inferno-5-g80: “Poscia ch’” forms a temporal conjunction, “after that/after.” “Ebbi ... udito” is an anterior past construction, “I had heard,” and “nomar” is an infinitive depending on “udito”: Dante heard his teacher name the ancient women and knights; “’l” is contracted “il,” and “’ cavalieri” stands for “i cavalieri.”
segments.inferno-5-g81: The subject is “pietà,” placed first for emphasis. “Mi giunse” literally means “came to me” or “overtook me,” expressing that pity suddenly seized Dante emotionally.
segments.inferno-5-g82: “E” joins the emotional result to the previous clause. “Fui” is the past tense of “essere,” and “smarrito” is a past participle used adjectivally, meaning confused, overcome, or bewildered; “quasi” softens it to “almost.”
segments.inferno-5-g83: “I’” is a poetic clipped form of “io,” “I,” not the article despite its appearance. “Cominciai” is first-person singular passato remoto, “I began,” introducing Dante’s direct speech.
segments.inferno-5-g84: “Poeta” is a vocative: Dante is directly addressing Virgil as “Poet.” The single-word segment opens the quoted request.
segments.inferno-5-g85: “Volontieri” modifies “parlerei,” giving “I would gladly speak”; “parlerei” is conditional, making the request courteous and tentative. “A quei due” means “to those two,” and “che ’nsieme vanno” is a relative clause, “who go together,” with “’nsieme” an apocopated form of “insieme.”
segments.inferno-5-g86: “E” adds a second relative description of the same two souls. “Paion ... esser leggeri” means “they seem to be light,” with “esser” as the infinitive after “paion”; “sì” is an intensifier, “so,” and “al vento” describes their lightness in relation to the wind that carries them.
segments.inferno-5-g66: “Ell’” is the elided form of “ella,” used before “è”; the pronoun refers to the female shade being identified. The clause has the simple subject–verb–complement structure “she is Semiramis.”
segments.inferno-5-g67: “Di cui” means “of whom,” introducing a relative clause about Semiramis. “Si legge” is an impersonal/passive construction, “one reads” or “it is written,” followed by the content clause “che succedette a Nino e fu sua sposa,” where the passato remoto verbs state completed historical facts.
segments.inferno-5-g68: “Tenne” is passato remoto of “tenere,” here meaning that she held or ruled the land. “Che ’l Soldan corregge” is a relative clause modifying “la terra”; “’l” is elided “il,” “Soldan” is the archaic form of “sultan,” and “corregge” means “governs.”
segments.inferno-5-g69: “L’altra” means “the other woman/shade,” continuing the list of famous souls. “Colei che” means “she who,” and “s’ancise” is a reflexive passato remoto form meaning “killed herself”; “amorosa” explains her state or motive, “in love.”
segments.inferno-5-g70: The conjunction “e” adds a second action by the same subject. “Ruppe fede” means “broke faith” or “violated fidelity,” and “al cener di Sicheo” means “to the ashes of Sychaeus,” with “cener” a poetic singular/shortened form for the dead man’s remains.
segments.inferno-5-g71: “Poi” marks the next figure in the enumeration, not a narrative sequence of events. “È” again means “is/comes,” and “lussurïosa” is an adjective attached to Cleopatra; the diaeresis in “lussurïosa” marks the syllables for the verse meter.
segments.inferno-5-g72: “Elena” is placed before the verb for emphasis as the direct object: literally, “Helen you see.” “Vedi” is second-person singular present/imperative addressed to Dante, meaning “you see” or “look at.”
segments.inferno-5-g73: “Per cui” means “for whom” or “for whose sake,” referring back to Helen. “Tanto reo tempo” is the subject of “si volse”; “si volse” is a reflexive/poetic passato remoto expression meaning that the evil period “turned” or “passed.”
segments.inferno-5-g74: The conjunction “e” coordinates a second command/statement of seeing with the earlier “Elena vedi.” “’l” is the elided article “il,” and “grande” means “great,” emphasizing Achilles’ heroic stature.
segments.inferno-5-g75: “Che” introduces a relative clause referring to Achilles. “Combatteo” is an archaic poetic form of “combatté,” passato remoto; “con amore” can be understood as “against Love” or “with Love” as the force/opponent involved, and “al fine” means “at the end.”
segments.inferno-5-g122: “Amor” is personified as the subject, and “condusse” is the passato remoto, a narrative past: “led.” The object pronoun “noi” follows the verb for emphasis, and “ad una morte” means “to one/single death,” stressing that the two lovers shared the same fate.
segments.inferno-5-g123: “Caina” is the subject, the infernal region reserved for traitors to kin, and “attende” is present tense: “awaits.” “Chi” functions as an indefinite relative, “he who / the one who,” while “ci spense” uses “spense,” passato remoto of “spegnere,” metaphorically “extinguished,” meaning “killed”; “a vita” is an archaic/poetic expression referring to life, i.e. depriving them of life.
segments.inferno-5-g124: “Queste parole” is the plural subject placed first: “these words.” “Ci” is an indirect object, “to us,” while “da lor” gives the agent, “by them”; “fuor porte” is an archaic poetic passive form, with “fuor” = “furono” and “porte” meaning “brought/presented,” not “gates.”
segments.inferno-5-g40: “Intesi” is a first-person past tense form meaning “I understood.” The clause introduced by “ch’” depends on it; “a così fatto tormento” is placed before the verb for emphasis, and “enno” is an archaic form of “sono,” so the construction is “are condemned to such a punishment.”
segments.inferno-5-g41: “Che” is a relative pronoun referring back to “i peccator carnali.” The verb “sommettono” is present tense, and “al” contracts “a” + “il”; the word order is straightforward: they submit “la ragion” to “il talento,” that is, reason to appetite or desire.
segments.inferno-5-g42: “E come” begins a simile: “And as.” In “li stornei ne portan l’ali,” the object “li stornei” is placed before the verb, while the subject “l’ali” follows it; “ne” has an old adverbial force, suggesting motion along or away, so the sense is that their wings carry the starlings.
segments.inferno-5-g43: “A schiera” is a manner phrase meaning “in a formation/flock.” The adjectives “larga e piena” agree with “schiera” and describe the flock as broad and dense or full.
segments.inferno-5-g44: “Così” completes the comparison begun by “come”: as the starlings are carried, so the spirits are driven. “Quel fiato” is the subject, “li spiriti mali” the object, and “di qua” begins a series of directional adverbs meaning “this way.”
segments.inferno-5-g45: “Di là” continues the directional series after “di qua.” The phrase means “that way” or “over there,” showing the erratic motion imposed by the wind.
segments.inferno-5-g46: “Di giù” adds a downward direction to the same series. The repeated “di” gives a rapid, buffeted rhythm to the movement.
segments.inferno-5-g47: “Di sù” completes the set with upward motion, and “li mena” gives the governing verb: the blast drives them. Here “li” is not an article but a direct-object pronoun, “them.”
segments.inferno-5-g48: “Nulla speranza” functions as the subject and means “no hope.” “Li conforta mai” has “li” as the direct object, “them,” and “mai” means “ever” within the negative idea, yielding “no hope ever comforts them.”
segments.inferno-5-g49: “Non che” is idiomatic: here it means “not to mention” or “let alone.” “Di posa” depends on the implied “speranza,” meaning a hope “of rest.”
segments.inferno-5-g50: “Ma di minor pena” contrasts with “di posa”: they have not even hope of rest, nor even of a lesser pain. “Minor” is the poetic shortened form of “minore” and modifies “pena.”
segments.inferno-5-g51: “E come” introduces another simile. “I gru” is an archaic or poetic article-noun combination for “the cranes,” and “van cantando” is a progressive construction, literally “go singing”; “lor lai” means “their laments.”
segments.inferno-5-g52: “Faccendo” is an archaic form of “facendo,” the gerund “making.” The phrase “di sé lunga riga” means “a long line of themselves,” with “di sé” indicating what the line is made from: the birds themselves in formation.
segments.inferno-5-g53: “Così” completes the comparison: as the cranes come in a line, so Dante sees the shades come. “Vid’ io” is inverted poetic word order for “io vidi,” and “venir” is the infinitive depending on “vidi”: “I saw [them] come.”
segments.inferno-5-g54: “Traendo” is a gerund modifying the coming shades. Literally “drawing out woes,” it means they are uttering or giving forth cries of pain.
segments.inferno-5-g55: “Ombre” identifies what Dante saw: the souls or shades of the dead. “Portate” is a passive participle agreeing with “ombre,” and “da la detta briga” means they are carried by “the aforesaid turmoil,” namely the storm already described.
segments.inferno-5-g56: “Per ch’i’” is a compressed poetic form of “per che io,” meaning “wherefore” or “because of which I.” “Dissi” is first-person past tense, introducing Dante’s direct speech.
segments.inferno-5-g57: “Maestro” is a vocative: Dante directly addresses Virgil. The word carries the sense of “master” or authoritative guide, not merely a schoolteacher.
segments.inferno-5-g58: “Chi son quelle genti” is a direct question: “who are those people?” The relative clause “che l’aura nera sì gastiga” has “che” as the object of “gastiga,” while “l’aura nera” is the subject: the dark air so chastises them.
segments.inferno-5-g125: “Quand’” is an elided form of “quando,” introducing a temporal subordinate clause. “Intesi” is passato remoto, first person singular, here meaning “I heard” or “I took in,” and “quell’ anime offense” means “those injured/wronged souls,” with the adjective “offense” agreeing with the plural feminine “anime.”
segments.inferno-5-g126: “China’” stands for “chinai,” passato remoto first person singular of “chinare,” with the final vowel elided poetically: “I bowed/lowered.” The object is “il viso,” literally “the face,” meaning Dante lowers his face or gaze.
segments.inferno-5-g127: “E” links this action to the previous one. “Tanto” has an adverbial force, “so much/so long,” while “il” is not the article here but a direct-object pronoun referring back to “viso”; “tenni basso” means “I kept it low.”
segments.inferno-5-g128: “Fin che” forms the conjunction “until,” introducing the point at which Dante’s silence or lowered gaze ends. “’l poeta” is the elided form of “il poeta,” and “mi disse” uses “mi” as an indirect object: “said to me.”
segments.inferno-5-g129: “Che” is interrogative here, meaning “what.” “Pense” is an archaic or poetic second-person singular form equivalent to modern Italian “pensi”: “what are you thinking?”
segments.inferno-5-g130: This segment contains only punctuation and has no grammatical words to analyze.
segments.inferno-5-g131: “Quando” introduces the temporal clause “when I answered.” “Rispuosi” is an archaic form of “risposi,” passato remoto first person singular, marking Dante’s reply after the poet’s question.
segments.inferno-5-g132: “Cominciai” is passato remoto first person singular, literally “I began.” In this context it means “I began to speak,” introducing Dante’s quoted response.
segments.inferno-5-g133: “Oh” is an exclamatory particle. “Lasso” is an adjective used interjectionally, meaning “wretched” or “unhappy,” so the phrase expresses pity and emotional distress.
segments.inferno-5-g134: “Quanti” is exclamatory and plural, agreeing with “pensier”: “how many thoughts.” “Pensier” is the poetic truncated form of “pensieri,” and “dolci” qualifies the thoughts as sweet or tender.
segments.inferno-5-g135: “Quanto disio” is a parallel exclamation to “quanti dolci pensier,” with “disio” an archaic form of “desiderio.” “Menò” is passato remoto third person singular, “led,” and “al” contracts “a + il”; “doloroso passo” means the painful passage or fateful step into ruin.
segments.inferno-5-g144: This target consists only of punctuation, marking the close of the preceding direct question; it contains no words to analyze.
segments.inferno-5-g145: E links the reply to the preceding question. quella is a demonstrative pronoun, “that one,” referring to the woman who answers; a me is an indirect-object phrase, with the verb of speaking left understood: “said to me.”
segments.inferno-5-g146: Nessun maggior dolore is an elliptical comparative construction: literally “no greater sorrow,” meaning “there is no greater sorrow.” che introduces the comparison, “than,” followed by the substantivized reflexive infinitive ricordarsi, “remembering”; ne la is the older separated spelling of nella, “in the.”
segments.inferno-5-g147: e adds a confirming clause to the maxim just stated. ciò refers back to that truth, sa is present indicative “knows,” and ’l is the elided form of il before tuo dottore, “your teacher,” referring to Dante’s guide.
segments.inferno-5-g148: Ma introduces a contrast with the pain of remembering: despite that pain, she will answer if Dante desires it. s’ is not reflexive here but the elided se, “if,” before a; a conoscer is an infinitive phrase dependent on hai cotanto affetto, “you have so great a desire to know.” amor is the poetic apocopated form of amore, and prima radice means the first origin or root cause.
segments.inferno-5-g149: dirò is future indicative, “I will tell/say,” completing the conditional sentence begun with Ma s’. come colui che introduces a simile, “as one who,” and the present indicatives piange and dice describe simultaneous action: speaking while weeping.
segments.inferno-5-g111: The verb comes first in the poetic order: “Siede la terra” means “the town sits/lies,” i.e. is situated. “Dove nata fui” is an inverted form of “dove fui nata,” with “fui” as auxiliary and “nata” agreeing with the female speaker; “su la” is the older separated form of “sulla,” and “’l” is elided “il.” “Per aver pace” expresses purpose, while “co’ seguaci sui” means “with its followers,” with “co’” = “con i” and “sui” used archaically for possessive “suoi,” referring to the Po’s tributaries.
segments.inferno-5-g112: “Amor” is personified as the grammatical subject of the sentence, governing the later verb “prese.” The form is poetic for “Amore,” and here it means Love as an active power.
segments.inferno-5-g113: This relative clause describes “Amor”: “ch’al” contracts “che” + “al,” and “cor” is the apocopated poetic form of “cuore.” “Gentil” follows the noun and means morally noble; “ratto” functions adverbially as “quickly,” and “s’apprende” is pronominal/reflexive, literally “attaches itself” or “takes hold.”
segments.inferno-5-g114: “Prese” is passato remoto, narrating the decisive past action: Love “seized” or overcame “costui,” the man indicated beside her. “De la” is the older separated form of “della” and here gives cause or occasion, “because of/from the beautiful body”; “persona” means the body. “Che mi fu tolta” is a relative clause modifying “persona,” with “fu tolta” as a passive passato remoto: “was taken away from me.”
segments.inferno-5-g115: The conjunction “e” coordinates a second main statement. “’L” is elided “il,” and “m’offende” is “mi offende,” with the object pronoun elided before the vowel; the present tense with “ancor” shows that the hurt continues even now.
segments.inferno-5-g116: “Amor” again stands as the personified subject, picking up the previous sentence’s pattern. It will govern the main verb “prese” later in the sentence.
segments.inferno-5-g117: This relative clause states a general law about Love: “ch’a” is “che” + “a,” and “a nullo amato” means “to no beloved person.” “Amar” is a truncated infinitive, “to love,” used as the thing from which Love does not “perdona,” that is, does not spare or exempt anyone.
segments.inferno-5-g118: The object pronoun “mi” precedes “prese,” so the sense is “seized me.” “Del costui piacer” is an archaic construction: literally “of the charm of this man,” meaning that his pleasing beauty or charm was the cause or means of Love’s seizure. “Sì forte” means “so strongly” and prepares the result clause introduced by the following “che.”
segments.inferno-5-g119: “Che” introduces the result clause dependent on “sì forte”: Love seized her “so strongly that...” It is not a relative pronoun here but a consecutive conjunction.
segments.inferno-5-g120: “Come vedi” is a parenthetical address to Dante: “as you see.” “Vedi” is second-person singular present indicative, directly involving the listener as witness.
segments.inferno-5-g121: This is the result of “sì forte”: Love still does not abandon her. “M’abbandona” is “mi abbandona,” with elision of the object pronoun before the vowel, and the present tense expresses the continuing power of that love.
segments.inferno-5-g136: This target segment consists only of a full stop, closing the preceding quoted exclamation. There are no lexical items or grammatical forms to analyze here.
segments.inferno-5-g137: “Poi” marks the next action in sequence. “Mi rivolsi a loro” is a reflexive/pronominal past-tense form meaning “I turned myself toward them,” with “a loro” giving the people addressed; “parla’ io” is poetic elision for “parlai io,” a first-person passato remoto, with “io” added explicitly for emphasis and meter.
segments.inferno-5-g138: “E cominciai” continues the sequence with another first-person passato remoto. Since it introduces direct speech, “cominciai” means “I began to speak” rather than merely “I started” in general.
segments.inferno-5-g139: “Francesca” is a vocative: Dante directly addresses the soul by name. The comma marks the pause after this direct address before the sentence continues.
segments.inferno-5-g140: “I tuoi martìri” is the subject of “fanno”: literally, “your torments make.” The construction “mi fanno tristo e pio” means “make me sad and compassionate,” while “a lagrimar” is an infinitive phrase, “to weep,” expressing the emotional result or tendency caused in him.
segments.inferno-5-g141: “Ma” turns from Dante’s expression of pity to a question. “Dimmi” is the imperative “di’” plus the enclitic pronoun “mi,” meaning “tell me.”
segments.inferno-5-g142: “Al tempo” means “at the time,” with “al” contracted from “a” + “il.” “D’i” is an archaic/poetic contraction for “di i,” equivalent to modern “dei”; the phrase “i dolci sospiri” evokes the early, tender signs of love.
segments.inferno-5-g143: “A che e come” asks literally “by what and how,” that is, by what means or signs and in what manner. “Concedette amore” uses inverted poetic order: the subject is personified “amore,” and “concedette” is passato remoto; “che conosceste” is a dependent clause meaning “that you should/could come to know,” with “conosceste” referring to the two lovers and “i dubbiosi disiri” to desires that were still uncertain or unconfessed.
segments.inferno-5-g97: Quali introduces the first half of a simile, answered later by cotali: “as ... so.” Vegnon is an archaic/poetic form of vengono, “they come”; dal disio chiamate and con l’ali alzate e ferme describe the colombe, while al dolce nido gives their destination and per l’aere the path through which they move.
segments.inferno-5-g98: Dal voler portate is a participial phrase agreeing with colombe: they are “carried” or impelled by their own will. Dal here expresses agency or cause, “by the will,” not motion from a place.
segments.inferno-5-g99: Cotali completes the comparison begun by quali: “such,” that is, like those doves. Uscir is a shortened poetic form for uscirono, “they came out/emerged,” and de la schiera ov’ è Dido means “from the band where Dido is,” with ov’ standing for ove, “where.”
segments.inferno-5-g100: A noi gives the direction, “toward us,” and venendo is a gerund that describes the manner or accompanying action of uscir: they came forth while coming toward Dante and Virgil. Per l’aere maligno repeats the idea of movement through the air, now marked as hellish or evil.
segments.inferno-5-g101: Sì forte fu gives the cause of their approach: “so strong was” the call. The noun phrase l’affettüoso grido is the subject after the verb; affettüoso is written with diaeresis to mark the syllables for the meter.
segments.inferno-5-g102: O marks direct address, and animal here means a living human creature, not merely a beast. Che introduces a relative clause describing Dante: visitando vai is literally “you go visiting,” and noi che tignemmo il mondo di sanguigno is the object of that visiting, “us who stained the world blood-red”; perso is a dark, dusky color, not “lost” here.
segments.inferno-5-g103: Se fosse introduces an unreal condition with the imperfect subjunctive fosse, “if ... were.” The subject il re de l’universo follows the predicate amico, a poetic word order meaning “if the King of the universe were friendly/a friend to us.”
segments.inferno-5-g104: Noi pregheremmo is the conditional main clause corresponding to se fosse: “we would pray.” Lui is the person prayed to, and de la tua pace is an archaic spelling of della tua pace, here meaning “for/concerning your peace.”
segments.inferno-5-g105: Poi c’hai is a contraction of poi che hai, meaning “since you have,” not a form with ci as an object. Hai pietà del nostro mal perverso means “you have pity for our perverse suffering/evil,” with del introducing what the pity is directed toward.
segments.inferno-5-g106: Di quel is fronted and depends on the following verbs: “about that which.” The two che forms mean “what/which,” as objects of udire and parlar; parlar is an apocopated infinitive for parlare, and vi is the dative with piace, “pleases you.”
segments.inferno-5-g107: Noi is explicit for emphasis: “we, for our part.” Udiremo and parleremo are future-tense verbs, coordinated by e, and a voi marks the addressee of the speaking.
segments.inferno-5-g108: Mentre che is a compound conjunction meaning “while” or “as long as.” ’L is the elided form of il before vento, “the wind,” which is the subject of the clause completed by tace.
segments.inferno-5-g109: Come fa is parenthetical: “as it does” or “as it is doing now.” Fa refers back to the wind’s present action or condition in this brief pause.
segments.inferno-5-g110: Ci is dative here, “for us” or “to us.” Tace literally means “is silent,” applied poetically to the wind to mean that it has fallen quiet or ceased for their benefit.
segments.inferno-5-g159: “Galeotto” is a predicate noun placed first for emphasis: the book and its author acted as the go-between who brought the lovers together. “Fu” is the passato remoto of “essere,” and “’l” is the contracted article “il”; “chi lo scrisse” means “he who wrote it,” with “lo” referring back to “libro.”
segments.inferno-5-g160: “Quel giorno” is an adverbial phrase of time, “that day.” The sequence “più non ... avante” means “no further / no longer onward”; “vi” is a locative pronoun meaning “there” or “in it,” referring to the book or the reading.
segments.inferno-5-g161: “Mentre che” is an older expanded form of “mentre,” meaning “while.” “L’uno spirto” means “the one spirit,” contrasted with “l’altro” in the next segment; “disse” is passato remoto, narrating the completed act of speaking.
segments.inferno-5-g162: “L’altro” completes the contrast begun by “l’uno spirto”: one spirit spoke, the other wept. “Piangëa” is a poetic form of “piangea,” the imperfect tense, describing an ongoing action: “was weeping.”
segments.inferno-5-g163: “Sì che” introduces a result clause, “so that.” “Di pietade” gives the cause, “from pity,” and “venni men” is the idiom “I fainted/swooned”; “com’ io morisse” uses the imperfect subjunctive “morisse” after an implicit “as if,” expressing comparison rather than actual death.
segments.inferno-5-g164: “E” links this final action to the preceding swoon. “Caddi” is passato remoto, “I fell,” while “come corpo morto cade” is a stark simile; the article is omitted before “corpo morto,” a poetic and generalizing construction meaning “as a dead body falls.”
segments.inferno-5-g150: The subject pronoun “Noi” is explicit for emphasis, and “leggiavamo” is an imperfect verb, describing an ongoing past activity: “we were reading.” “Di Lancialotto” means “about/of Lancelot,” and “come amor lo strinse” is an indirect-content clause, “how Love constrained him”; “lo” is the direct-object pronoun, though its listed senses do not include that pronominal use.
segments.inferno-5-g151: “Soli eravamo” uses inverted poetic word order: adjective/predicate first, then the imperfect verb, meaning “we were alone.” “Sanza” is an archaic form of modern Italian “senza,” and “alcun” after “sanza” means “any,” giving “without any suspicion.”
segments.inferno-5-g152: “Per più fïate” is an archaic/poetic expression meaning “several times” or “on several occasions.” The word order is inverted: the object phrase “li occhi” comes before the verb “sospinse,” while “quella lettura” is the subject placed after the verb; “ci” functions like a dative of possession, so “li occhi ci” means “our eyes.”
segments.inferno-5-g153: “Scolorocci” is a compact poetic form for “scolorò ci,” literally “it made pale for us,” with “ci” absorbed into the verb. “Il viso” is singular in form but refers to the face/appearance of the two lovers collectively, hence naturally “our faces.”
segments.inferno-5-g154: “Ma” marks a contrast with the repeated earlier reactions: despite many emotional stirrings, only one “punto,” one point or moment in the reading, truly overcame them. “Fu quel che ci vinse” literally means “was that which overcame us,” with “che” as a relative pronoun and “ci” as the direct object “us.”
segments.inferno-5-g155: “Quando leggemmo” uses the preterite, marking the decisive moment in the past: “when we read.” “Il disïato riso esser basciato” is an accusative-and-infinitive-like construction: literally “the desired smile to be kissed,” meaning “that the desired smile was kissed”; “da cotanto amante” gives the agent of the passive, “by so great a lover.”
segments.inferno-5-g156: “Questi” means “this one” or “this man,” pointing to Paolo, who is present beside Francesca as she speaks. It serves as the subject of the main verb that comes later, “basciò.”
segments.inferno-5-g157: This is a relative clause modifying “questi”: “who will never be separated from me.” “Fia” is an archaic future form meaning “will be,” and “mai ... non” is a medieval Italian double-negative construction with the force of “never.”
segments.inferno-5-g158: This is the main clause resumed after the interruption: “la bocca mi basciò” literally means “he kissed the mouth to me,” where “mi” is a dative of possession, naturally “my mouth.” “Tutto tremante” means “all trembling” or “completely trembling,” and it describes “questi,” the kissing lover, not the mouth.

sentences.inferno-5-s4: Always many of them stand before him: they go one by one to judgment; they speak and hear, and then are turned downward.
sentences.inferno-5-s5: “O you who come to this sorrowful lodging,” Minos said to me when he saw me, leaving the act of so great an office, “take heed how you enter and in whom you trust; let not the spaciousness of the entrance deceive you!”
sentences.inferno-5-s7: And my guide said to him: “Why do you still cry out?”
sentences.inferno-5-s8: Do not hinder his fated journey: it is willed so there where what is willed can be done, and ask no more.”
sentences.inferno-5-s9: Now the sorrowful sounds begin to make themselves heard by me; now I have come to the place where much weeping strikes me.
sentences.inferno-5-s1: Thus I descended from the first circle down into the second, which girds less space and so much more pain that it stings one to wailing.
sentences.inferno-5-s2: There Minos stands horribly, and snarls: he examines the sins at the entrance; he judges and sends according as he coils himself.
sentences.inferno-5-s3: I say that when the ill-born soul comes before him, it confesses itself completely; and that knower of sins sees what place in Hell is for it; he girds himself with his tail as many times as the number of degrees he wants it to be sent down.
sentences.inferno-5-s10: I came into a place mute of every light, which roars as the sea does in a storm, when it is beaten by opposing winds.
sentences.inferno-5-s11: The hellish whirlwind, which never rests, drives the spirits along with its violent seizure; turning and striking them, it torments them.
sentences.inferno-5-s12: Whenever they come before the ruin, there are shrieks there, lamentation, and wailing; there they curse the divine power.
sentences.inferno-5-s25: And he said to me: “You will see when they are nearer to us; and then entreat them by that love which leads them, and they will come.”
sentences.inferno-5-s26: As soon as the wind turned them toward us, I raised my voice: “O troubled souls, come speak with us, if another does not forbid it!”
sentences.inferno-5-s17: “The first of those about whom you wish to know news,” he then said to me, “was empress over many tongues.”
sentences.inferno-5-s18: She was so given over to the vice of lust that she made desire lawful in her law, in order to take away the blame into which she had been brought.
sentences.inferno-5-s22: “Behold Paris, Tristan”; and he showed me and named to me, pointing with his finger, more than a thousand shades whom love had parted from our life.
sentences.inferno-5-s23: After I had heard my teacher name the ancient ladies and the knights, pity came over me, and I was almost bewildered.
sentences.inferno-5-s24: I began: “Poet, I would gladly speak to those two who go together and seem to be so light upon the wind.”
sentences.inferno-5-s19: She is Semiramis, of whom one reads that she succeeded Ninus and was his wife; she held the land that the Sultan governs.
sentences.inferno-5-s20: The other is she who killed herself for love, and broke faith with the ashes of Sychaeus; then comes lustful Cleopatra.
sentences.inferno-5-s21: You see Helen, for whose sake such a long evil time passed, and you see great Achilles, who at the end fought against Love.
sentences.inferno-5-s34: Love led us to one death.
sentences.inferno-5-s35: Caina awaits the one who ended our lives.
sentences.inferno-5-s36: These words were brought to us by them.
sentences.inferno-5-s13: I understood that to such a punishment are condemned the carnal sinners, who subject reason to desire.
sentences.inferno-5-s14: And as starlings are borne by their wings in the cold season, in a broad and crowded flock, so that blast drives the evil spirits here and there, down and up; no hope ever comforts them—not hope of rest, nor even of lesser pain.
sentences.inferno-5-s15: And as cranes go singing their laments, making a long line of themselves in the air, so I saw shades coming, uttering cries of woe, carried along by the aforesaid turmoil; wherefore I said: “Master, who are those people whom the black air so chastises?”
sentences.inferno-5-s37: When I heard those wounded souls, I lowered my face, and held it so low until the poet said to me, “What are you thinking?”
sentences.inferno-5-s39: When I answered, I began: “Ah, wretched me, how many sweet thoughts, how much desire led these people to the painful pass!”
sentences.inferno-5-s44: And she said to me: “There is no greater sorrow than to remember a happy time in misery; and this your teacher knows.”
sentences.inferno-5-s45: But if you have so great a desire to know the first root of our love, I will speak as one who weeps and speaks.
sentences.inferno-5-s31: The town where I was born lies on the seashore where the Po flows down to find rest with its followers.
sentences.inferno-5-s32: Love, which swiftly takes hold of a noble heart, seized this man because of the beautiful body that was taken from me; and the manner of it still hurts me.
sentences.inferno-5-s33: Love, which spares no beloved person from loving, seized me with this man’s charm so strongly that, as you see, it still does not leave me.
sentences.inferno-5-s41: Then I turned to them and spoke, and began: “Francesca, your torments make me sad and compassionate, even to tears.”
sentences.inferno-5-s42: But tell me: at the time of the sweet sighs, by what and how did Love grant that you should recognize the uncertain desires?
sentences.inferno-5-s28: As doves, summoned by longing, with wings raised and steady, come through the air to the sweet nest, carried by their will, so those two came forth from the band where Dido is, coming toward us through the evil air, so strong was the affectionate call.
sentences.inferno-5-s29: “O gracious and kindly living creature, who, going on your visit through the dusky air, come to us who stained the world blood-red: if the King of the universe were friendly to us, we would pray to him for your peace, since you have pity for our perverse suffering.
sentences.inferno-5-s30: Concerning whatever it pleases you to hear and to speak about, we will listen and speak to you, while the wind, as it does now, is silent for us.
sentences.inferno-5-s49: The book, and the man who wrote it, was a Galeotto for us: that day we read no further in it.
sentences.inferno-5-s50: While the one spirit was saying this, the other was weeping; so that, from pity, I swooned as if I were dying.
sentences.inferno-5-s51: And I fell as a dead body falls.
sentences.inferno-5-s46: One day we were reading, for pleasure, about Lancelot and how Love constrained him; we were alone and without any suspicion.
sentences.inferno-5-s47: Several times that reading impelled our eyes, and it made our faces pale; but only one point was what overcame us.
sentences.inferno-5-s48: When we read that the desired smile was kissed by so great a lover, this man, who will never be separated from me, kissed my mouth, all trembling.
